overview.body: Work through each Gaddis program in order. Type every program by hand — the repetition is how the syntax becomes second nature. These ten short programs cover the fundamentals you will use in every Lab assignment this semester.
objectives: Declare variables and observe reassignment in action
Collect user input using input() with int() and float() conversion
Perform arithmetic calculations and display results
Convert seconds to hours, minutes, and seconds using // and %
Format output with f-strings, including decimal precision and column alignment
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 2 covers variables, input(), type conversion, arithmetic operators, integer division (//), modulo (%), string concatenation, and f-string formatting with field width and precision specifiers.
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 2: Input, Processing, and Output
programRequirements: Each program is saved as its own individual .py file
Each file uses the exact filename listed in the instructions
Each program runs without syntax errors
Output matches what is shown in the textbook
overview.filenameNote: This chapter uses 10 individual files. Submit all 10 files together in your final CodeGrade submission.

firstTimeCodeGrade.title: First Time Submitting to CodeGrade?
firstTimeCodeGrade.body: This is your first CodeGrade assignment. When you click Submit, you will be prompted to create your CodeGrade account and enter payment. This is a one-time setup — all future submissions go straight through. Have one of these ready: a credit card to pay CodeGrade directly, or an activation code from the UTSA Bookstore.

content.body: A short program that creates a variable, prints its value, reassigns it to a new value, and prints it again. This demonstrates that a variable name is a label that can point to different values over time.

items: Open your textbook to Program 2-10.
Create a new file named variable_demo4.py.
Type the program exactly as shown in the book.
Run the file and confirm the two output lines match the book.
Fix any typos until it runs cleanly.

content.title: Build and Test After Each Checkpoint
content.body: Don't write all 10 programs at once. Get each file working before moving to the next. This habit will save you hours of debugging later.

A program that collects a name, age, and income from the user using input(), converts the numeric values with int() and float(), and prints them back. This is the core pattern behind every program that asks users for data.

items: Open your textbook to Program 2-13.
Create a new file named input.py.
Type the code exactly as shown.
Run the file and enter test values when prompted.
Confirm the output matches the book.

content.title: input() Always Returns a String
content.body: Even if the user types a number, input() returns it as text. Wrap it in int() for whole numbers or float() for decimals, or your math will not work.

A program that assigns a salary and bonus to variables, adds them together, stores the result in a third variable, and prints it. Simple arithmetic, but it introduces the pattern of storing calculation results in variables.

items: Open your textbook to Program 2-14.
Create a new file named simple_math.py.
Type the code as shown.
Run it and confirm the calculated total is correct.
Fix any issues until output matches the book.

content.title: Use Clear Variable Names
content.body: The book uses salary, bonus, and pay — not x, y, and z. Descriptive names make your code readable and are required for full credit on Lab assignments.

A program that collects three test scores from the user, calculates their average, and displays the result. This ties together input(), float() conversion, arithmetic, and print() — the exact workflow you will use in your Lab assignments.

items: Open your textbook to Program 2-16.
Create a new file named test_score_average.py.
Type the full example from the book.
Run the program and verify the average is calculated correctly.
Test it a second time with different input values.

content.title: Use / Not // for Averages
content.body: Integer division (//) rounds down and will give you a wrong average. Use regular division (/) when you need a decimal result.

A program that takes a total number of seconds and converts it to hours, remaining minutes, and remaining seconds using integer division (//) and modulo (%). These two operators come up constantly throughout the rest of the semester.

items: Open your textbook to Program 2-17.
Create a new file named time_converter.py.
Type the code exactly as shown.
Run it using a value like 3661 seconds.
Verify the hours, minutes, and seconds output is correct.

content.title: // vs %
content.body: // gives you the quotient (how many times it divides evenly). % gives you the remainder. For 3661 seconds: 3661 // 3600 = 1 hour, 3661 % 3600 = 61 remaining seconds.

A program that collects a first and last name from the user, joins them together with a space using the + operator, and prints the full name. String concatenation was the original way to build output before f-strings existed, and it is still useful for simple joins.

items: Open your textbook to Program 2-19.
Create a new file named concatenation.py.
Type the code from the book.
Run it and verify the joined name appears correctly.
Notice that the space between names must be explicitly included as ' '.

content.title: You Cannot Concatenate a String and a Number
content.body: Using + to join a string and an int raises a TypeError. Use str() to convert the number first, or switch to an f-string.

A program that calculates a monthly payment and displays it using an f-string. No formatting specifier is used yet — just the basic f'...{variable}...' syntax. This is the modern, preferred way to build output in Python.

items: Open your textbook to Program 2-20.
Create a new file named f_string_no_formatting.py.
Type the code, paying attention to the f prefix before the opening quote.
Run and verify the variable value appears in the output.
Try changing the variable value to see it update in the print output.

content.title: Don't Forget the f Prefix
content.body: Without the f before the quote, Python prints the literal text {monthly_payment} instead of the value. This is an easy mistake and a common one.

The same monthly payment program as Checkpoint 7, but now with :.2f inside the curly braces to round the output to two decimal places. You will use this pattern in almost every Lab assignment this semester to display currency and calculated values cleanly.

items: Open your textbook to Program 2-21.
Create a new file named f_string_rounding.py.
Type the code, noting the :.2f inside the curly braces.
Run and verify the number is rounded to two decimal places.
Try changing :.2f to :.4f and see what happens.

content.title: :.2f Means Two Decimal Places
content.body: The colon starts the format spec, .2 sets precision to 2 decimal places, and f means floating-point. The entire spec goes inside the curly braces: {monthly_payment:.2f}.

A program that prints six decimal values arranged in two right-aligned columns using the :10.2f format specifier. The 10 reserves a field of 10 characters; the .2f rounds to two decimal places. The result is a clean, aligned table.

items: Open your textbook to Program 2-23.
Create a new file named columns.py.
Type the code carefully, paying close attention to the format specifiers.
Run the program and verify the numbers form neat aligned columns.
Compare your output to the book to confirm alignment is correct.

content.title: Field Width Controls Spacing
content.body: The number before the decimal in the format spec (10 in :10.2f) is the field width. All values right-align within that field, which is what creates the column effect.

A program that prints six names, each centered in a 20-character field using the :^20 format specifier. The *** markers on each side let you see exactly how wide the field is and confirm the text is centered.

items: Open your textbook to Program 2-24.
Create a new file named center_align.py.
Type the example and confirm the output is centered.
Try changing :^20 to :<20 (left) and :>20 (right) to see the difference.

content.title: Three Alignment Specifiers
content.body: ^ centers text, < left-aligns text, and > right-aligns text (the default for numbers). All three go inside the curly braces before the field width number.

beforeYouSubmit: variable_demo4.py runs and output matches the book
input.py runs and output matches the book
simple_math.py runs and output matches the book
test_score_average.py runs and output matches the book
time_converter.py runs and output matches the book
concatenation.py runs and output matches the book
f_string_no_formatting.py runs and output matches the book
f_string_rounding.py runs and output matches the book
columns.py runs and output matches the book
center_align.py runs and output matches the book
finalChecklist.submissionReminder: CodeGrade only keeps your most recent submission. Your final upload must include all 10 files or you will lose credit for any that are missing.
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: These are Chapter 2 BookEx exercises. You are typing Gaddis programs exactly as written. No functions, loops, or techniques from later chapters are needed or expected.

specific: int() and float() for type conversion
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: if/elif/else, loops, functions, lists — anything from chapters not yet covered

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read the checkpoint and compare your code to the book line by line — a typo is almost always the culprit
Run the program after each checkpoint rather than writing all 10 at once
Submit to CodeGrade early — it shows exactly which files pass and which need work
Post in the course Discord if you are stuck after two attempts (describe the error, do not post your code)
Come to Zoom or office hours if you are still stuck
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 2 BookEx Reference Guide — step-by-step breakdown of every program in this chapter
